content.full_text:
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

September 22, 2008

Councilman Don McCone
City of Needles
3520 Needles Hwy
Needles, CA 92363

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. I-08-151

Dear Councilmember McCone:

This letter responds to your request for advice regarding the conflict-of-interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹ You should not construe this letter as assessing any conduct that may have already taken place. (See Regulation 18329(b)(8)(A).) Because you seek general guidance, we provide you with informal assistance.²

In addition, we base this letter on the facts presented; the Fair Political Practices Commission (the "Commission") does not act as a finder of fact when it renders assistance. (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71.) Please note that we base our advice solely on the provisions of the Act and offer no advice on how other state laws or local laws might affect you, including common law conflict of interest and Section 1090. We recommend that you seek advice from your agency's counsel on the application of conflict of interest laws outside the Act.

QUESTION

Does your ownership interest and management position in a Needles R.V. Park or your ownership of several rental homes present a disqualifying conflict of interest regarding your potential participation in the making of an intergovernmental agency agreement with an Indian tribe concerning public safety and other impacts that will be necessary if the voters approve an Indian gaming facility?

___________________________

¹ The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated.

² Informal assistance does not offer the immunity provided by a Commission opinion or formal written advice. (Regulation 18329(c)(3) copy enclosed.)

File No. I-08-151
Page No. 2

CONCLUSION

Based on the facts as you have presented them, if you participate in making the
intergovernmental agency agreement, there do not appear to be any reasonably
foreseeable material financial effects on your economic interests that would create a
disqualifying conflict of interest. As discussed below, however, once the detail and
nature of the decisions to be made on the intergovernmental agency agreement become
more specific, you will have to reassess whether you have a conflict of interest.

FACTS

You are a city councilmember for the city of Needles. On the local ballot in
November, the people of Needles will decide whether to approve a new Indian gaming
facility ("casino") outside the city limit along Interstate 40. An affirmative vote in
November is necessary before the gaming facility can obtain final approval from the state
or federal governments. After this, the city must enter into an inter-governmental agency
agreement with the Indian tribe that will mitigate public safety and other impacts. The
city council, and sub-committees thereof, will be discussing the mitigations and public
safety impacts if the public approves the ballot measure and making decisions regarding
the agreement. You would like to participate.

You own 13 single-family, residential rental units in the city of Needles. You are
also a minority share holder and the manager of a permanent mobile home park and
seasonal riverfront R.V. Park (the Park"). The Park gets over 80% of its income from
permanent resident mobile homes and about 18% of its income from permanent seasonal
resort customers. There are currently six "transient" occupancy R.V. parks within a mile
and a half from the Park, meaning that these parks accept primarily overnight, short-term
guests.

The Indian Tribe that proposes building the gaming facility has also expressed an
interest in building a hotel, truck stop, and 50-space RV park. The city council would not
be involved in the decision-making on these issues; they are contingent on gaining
approval from the people of Needles and the casino project moving forward.

ANALYSIS

The Act's conflict-of-interest provisions ensure that public officials "perform
their duties in an impartial manner, free from bias caused by their own financial interests
or the financial interests of persons who have supported them." (Section 81001(b).)
Specifically, Section 87100 prohibits any public official from making, participating in
making, or otherwise using his or her official position to influence a governmental
decision in which the official has a financial interest.

The Commission has adopted an eight-step standard analysis to decide whether an
official has a disqualifying conflict of interest. (Regulation 18700(b)(1)-(8).) The
general rule, however, is that a conflict of interest exists whenever a public official makes
a governmental decision that has a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on one
or more of his or her financial interests. (Section 87103.)

File No. I-08-151
Page No. 3

Step One: Are you a public official?

As a member of the Needles City Council, you are a public official under the Act
(Section 82048.)

Step Two: Will you be making, participating in making, or using or
attempting to use his official position to influence a governmental decision?

The city council will be voting on whether to approve an inter-governmental
agency agreement between the city and the Mojave Indian Tribe. Sub-committees of the
council will be discussing particular issues involving the agreement and will report back
to the city council. By voting on or otherwise participating in the city's decision on the
agreement, you will be making, participating in making, or otherwise using your official
position to influence a governmental decision.³

Step Three: Do you have a financial interest in the decisions at issue?

A public official has a "financial interest" in a governmental decision within the
meaning of the Act if it is reasonably foreseeable that the governmental decision will
have a material financial effect, distinguishable from its effect on the public generally, on
the official, a member of his or her immediate family, or on any one of five enumerated
economic interests. (Section 87103; Regulation 18700(a).) The applicable economic
interests include:

1. An interest in a business entity in which a public official
has a direct or indirect investment of $2,000 or more.
(Section 87103(a), Regulation 18703.1(a).) An interest in any
business entity⁴ in which a public official is a director, officer,
partner, trustee, employee, or holds any position of
management. (Section 87103(d), Regulation 18703.1(b).)

2. An interest in real property in which a public official has
a direct or indirect interest of $2,000 or more. (Section
87103(b), Regulation 18703.2.)

3. Any source of income, including promised income, to the
public official that aggregates to $500 or more within 12
months prior to the decision. (Section 87103(c), Regulation
18703.3.)

³ When a public official who holds an office specified in section 87200 has a conflict of interest in
a decision noticed at a public meeting, then he or she must: (1) immediately prior to the discussion of the
item, orally identify each type of economic interest involved in the decision as well as details of the
economic interest, as discussed in regulation 18702.5(b), on the record of the meeting; (2) recuse himself or
herself; and (3) leave the room for the duration of the discussion and/or vote on the item. For closed
sessions, written calendars, absences and speaking as a member of the public regarding personal interests,
special rules found in regulation 18702.5(c) and 18702.5(d) apply.

⁴ Section 82005 provides: "Business entity" means any organization or enterprise operated for
profit, including but not limited to a proprietorship, partnership, firm, business trust, joint venture,
syndicate, corporation, or association. This definition does not include nonprofits or governmental entities.

File No. I-08-151
Page No. 4

4. Any source of gifts to the public official if the gifts
aggregate to $390 or more within 12 months prior to the
decision. (Section 87103(e), Regulation 18703.4.)

5. A public official also has an economic interest in his or
her personal expenses, income, assets, or liabilities, as well as
those of his or her immediate family. This is also known as
the "personal financial effects" rule. (Section 87103,
Regulation 18703.5.)

R.V. Park

Business Entity

You have an economic interest in a business entity if you have a direct or indirect
investment of $2,000 or more or if you have a position of management. You stated that
you are a minority shareholder in the Park, but you did not state whether your interest
equals $2,000 or more. Regardless of your ownership interest, the Park is a business
entity economic interest for you by virtue of your position of management.

Source of Income

If you receive income of $500 or more from the Park during the 12-month period
prior to your participation in a decision on the inter-governmental agency agreement, the
Park is also a source of income to you for purposes of the Act.

Furthermore, if you have a 10% ownership interest or more in the Park, the Park's
clients are sources of income to you if the percentage of their yearly fees that is equal to
your percent ownership totals more than $500 in the 12 months prior to your participation
in the intergovernmental decision. If this is the case, you will need to determine which of
the Park's residents are sources of income to you.

Rental Units

Real Property

You have an interest in your rental properties in Needles if your interest in each is
worth $2,000 or more.

Source of Income

Section 82030 includes rent payments under the definition of income. (See
Section 82030(a).) If any tenant pays you $500 or more within 12 months prior to your
making a governmental decision, that tenant is an economic interest. (Section 87103(c);
Regulation 18703.3.)

Personal Financial Effects

A public official always has an economic interest in his or her personal finances.
In particular, a government decision has a personal financial effect on a public official if
the decision will result in the personal expenses, income, assets, or liabilities of the
official increasing or decreasing. (Section 87103; Regulation 18703.5.)

Because you have not provided any information regarding other potential
economic interests, our analysis is limited to the interests discussed above.

File No. I-08-151
Page No. 5

Step Four: Is the economic interest directly or indirectly involved in the
governmental decision?

To determine whether a governmental decision's reasonably foreseeable financial
effect on an economic interest is material or not, you first must determine whether the
interest is directly or indirectly involved. (Regulation 18704(a).) For governmental
decisions that affect real property interests, the standards set forth in Regulation 18704.2
apply. (Regulation 18704(a)(2).) For governmental decisions that affect business entities
and sources of income, the standards set forth in Regulation 18704.1 apply. (Regulation
18704(a)(1).) For governmental decisions that affect the personal expenses, income,
assets or liabilities, the standards set for in Regulation 18704.5 apply. (Regulation
18704.5.)

Real Property

Regulation 18704.2(a) states, in pertinent part:

(a) Real property in which a public official has an economic
interest is directly involved in a governmental decision if any of
the following apply:

(1) The real property in which the official has an interest, or any
part of that real property, is located within 500 feet of the
boundaries (or the proposed boundaries) of the property which is
the subject of the governmental decision.

(2) The governmental decision involves the zoning or rezoning,
annexation or deannexation, sale, purchase, or lease, or inclusion
in or exclusion from any city, county, district or other local
governmental subdivision, of the real property in which the official
has an interest or a similar decision affecting the real property.

(3) The governmental decision involves the issuance, denial or
revocation of a license, permit or other land use entitlement
authorizing a specific use or uses of the real property in which the
official has an interest.

(4) The governmental decision involves the imposition, repeal or
modification of any taxes or fees assessed or imposed on the real
property in which the official has an interest.

(5) The governmental decision is to designate the survey area, to
select the project area, to adopt the preliminary plan, to form a
project area committee, to certify the environmental document, to
adopt the redevelopment plan, to add territory to the
redevelopment area, or to rescind or amend any of the above
decisions; and real property in which the official has an interest, or
any part of it is located within the boundaries (or the proposed
boundaries) of the redevelopment area.

File No. 1-08-151
Page No. 6

(6) The decision involves construction of, or improvements to,
streets, water, sewer, storm drainage or similar facilities, and the
real property in which the official has an interest will receive new
or improved services.

You have stated that your rental properties are about four to six miles from the
area that the city council's decisions will affect. Based on this fact, and the criteria in
Regulation 18704.2(a), your properties do not seem to be directly involved in the
decisions at issue at this time. The properties are therefore indirectly involved.

For indirectly involved real property interests, the materiality standard in
Regulation 18705.2 applies.

Business Entity and Source of Income

Regulation 18704.1(a) states:

(a) A person, including business entities, sources of income, and sources
of gifts, is directly involved in a decision before an official's agency when
that person, either directly or by an agent:

(1) Initiates the proceeding in which the decision will be made by filing an
application, claim, appeal, or similar request or;

(2) Is a named party in, or is the subject of, the proceeding concerning the
decision before the official or the official's agency. A person is the subject
of a proceeding if a decision involves the issuance, renewal, approval,
denial or revocation of any license, permit, or other entitlement to, or
contract with, the subject person.

Your facts do not suggest that the Park is directly involved in the governmental
decisions at issue.

If the Park's clients who you have identified as sources of income are involved in
the ways listed in Regulation 18704.1(a), you might have a directly involved economic
interest. Based on your facts, this is not the case. The same would apply if the people
who are renting your rental units are involved in the governmental decisions in the ways
described in Regulation 18704.1(a). Based on the facts you have provided, you do not
appear to have a directly involved economic interest based on income from these sources.

For indirectly involved business entities, the materiality standard set forth in
Regulation 18705.1(c) applies. (Regulation 18704.1(b).) (See Step 5 below for
discussion of the applicability of Regulation 18705.1.)

Personal Financial Effects

A public official is directly involved in a governmental decision if the decision
has any financial effect on his or her personal finances or those of his or her immediate
family. (Regulation 18704.5.) Again, your facts do not suggest that your personal

File No. I-08-151
Page No. 7

finances are involved with the governmental decision at issue. If, however, you are
making or participating in a decision that has any effect on your personal finances, the
conclusion would be different.

Step Five: Materiality Standard

A conflict of interest arises only when the reasonably foreseeable financial effect
of a governmental decision on a public official's economic interest is material.
(Regulation 18700(a).)

Real Property

The materiality standard for indirectly involved real property is set forth in
Regulation 18705.2(b)(1). The financial effect of a governmental decision on indirectly
involved real property is presumed not to be material. This presumption may be rebutted
by proof that there are specific circumstances regarding the governmental decision, its
financial effect, and the nature of the real property in which the public official has an
economic interest that make it reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a
material financial effect on the real property in which the public official has an interest.

Based on the information you provided, the effect of the governmental decisions
on your rental properties would not be material.

Business Entity

For business entities not directly involved in the governmental decision,
Regulation 18705.1 provides materiality standards that vary according to the financial
size of the business. For relatively small businesses (those with net income of less than
$500,000 for the most recent fiscal year), the materiality standards set forth in Regulation
18705.1(c)(4) apply:

"(4) If the business entity is not covered by subdivisions (c)(1)-(3), the
financial effect of a governmental decision on the business entity is
material if it is reasonably foreseeable that:

"(A) The governmental decision will result in an increase or decrease in
the business entity's gross revenues for a fiscal year in the amount of
$20,000 or more; or,

"(B) The governmental decision will result in the business entity incurring
or avoiding additional expenses or reducing or eliminating existing
expenses for a fiscal year in the amount of $5,000 or more; or,

"(C) The governmental decision will result in an increase or decrease in
the value of the business entity's assets or liabilities of $20,000 or more."

You have stated that the decisions that will come before the city council involve
mitigation efforts for a section of Needles that is about 6-7 miles from the Park. The city
council will not be deciding whether the casino will be built, but rather if the casino is
built, what effect it will have on Needles' population and services and what efforts will
need to be taken to mitigate the adverse effects. Because of the distance of the casino
from your business and the nature of the decisions you will be making, it would appear
that there would be no material financial impact on the business. You have not provided,

File No. I-08-151
Page No. 8

however, any specific information with regard to the governmental decisions
contemplated. Therefore, if you determine that the decisions before the Needles City
Council will have the effects listed above, then the effect of the governmental decisions
will be material.

Step Six: Reasonably Foreseeable

An effect upon economic interests is considered "reasonably foreseeable" if there
is a substantial likelihood that it will occur. (Regulation 18706(a).) Whether the
financial consequences of a governmental decision are substantially likely at the time the
decision is made depends on the facts surrounding the decision. A financial effect need
not be certain to be considered reasonably foreseeable, but it must be more than a mere
possibility. (In re Thorner (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 198.)

Ultimately, it is up to the public official to assess, through a good faith effort,
whether the effects of the decision by using some reasonable and objective method of
valuation. (Hensley Advice Letter, No. A-07-113; Moock Advice Letter, No. A-01-140;
O'Harra Advice Letter, No. A-00-174.) You will have to determine whether it is
reasonably foreseeable that the decisions before the city council will have a material
financial effect on any of your financial interests.

Steps Seven and Eight: Public Generally and Legally Required Participation

You have not presented any facts indicating that either the "public generally" or
the "legally required participation" exception would be applicable here. Accordingly, we
have not provided an analysis involving these steps.

If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

Scott Hallabrin
General Counsel

[signature]

By: Heather M. Rowan
Counsel, Legal Division

HMR:jgl

Because you have not provided any information regarcling other potential

Step Four: Is the economic interest directly or indirectly involved in the
governmental decision?
To determine whether a governmental decision's reasonably foreseeable financial
effect on an economic interest is material or not, you first must determine whether the
interest is directly or indirectly involved. (Regulation 1870 (a).) For governmenral
decisions that affect real property interests, the standards set forth in Rlgulation Lg7O4.z
apply' (Regulation 1870a@)Q).) For govemmental decisions that affect business entities
and sources of income, the standards set forth in Regulation lB7O4.1 apply. (Regulation
1870a(aXl).) For governmental decisions that affecl the personal e*penses, ir"o-",
assets or liabilities, the srandards set for in Regulation 1gr04.5 apply. (Regulation
18704.5.)
Real Property
Regulation 18704.2(a) states, in pertinent part:
(a) Real property in which a pubric official has an economic
interest is directly involved in a govemmental decision if any of
the following apply:
( I ) The real property in which the official has an interest, or any
part of that real property, is located within 500 feet of the
boundaries (or the proposed boundaries) of the property which is
the subject of the governmental decision
(2) The govemmental decision involves the zoning or rezoning,
annexation or deannexation, sale, purchase, or leaie, or inclusion
in or exclusion from any city, county, district or other local
governmental subdivision, of the real property in which the official
has an interest or a similar decision affecting the real property.
(3) The govemmental decision involves the issuance, denial or
revocation of a license, permit or other Iand use entitlement
authorizing a specific use or uses of the real property in which the
official has an interest.
(4) The governmental decision involves the imposition, repeal or
modification of any taxes or fees assessed or imposed on the real
properry in which the official has an interest.
(5) The governmental decision is to designate the survey area, to
select the project area, to adopt the preliminary plan, to form a
project area committee, to certify the environmental document, to
adopt the redevelopment plan, to add territory to the
redevelopment area, or to rescind or amend any of the above
decisions; and real property in which the official has an interest, or
any part of it is located within the boundaries (or the proposed
boundaries) of the redevelopment area.
(6) The decision involves constmction of. or improvements to,
streets. water, sewer, storm drainage or similar facilities, and the
real property in which the official has an interest will receive new
or improved services.
You have stated that your rental properties are about four to six miles from the
area that the city council's decisions will affect. Based on this fact, and the criteria in
Regulation 18704.2(a), your properties do not seem to be directly involved in the
decisions at issue at this time. The properties are therefore indirectly involved.
For indirectly involved real property interests, the materiality standard in
Regtrlation 187 05.2 applies.
Business Entity and Source of Income
Regulation 18704.1(a) srates:
(a) A person, including business entities, sources of income, and sources
of gifts, is directly involved in a decision before an official's agency when
that person, either directly or by an agent:
(l) Initiates the proceeding in which the decision will be made by filing an
application, claim, appeal, or similar request or;
(2) Is a named party in, or is the subject of, the proceeding concerning the
decision before the official or the official's agency. A person is the sribject
of a proceeding if a decision involves the issuance, renewal, approval,
denial or revocation of any ricense, permit, or other entitlemenito, or
contract with, the subject person.
Your facts do not suggest that the Park is directly involved in the govemmental
decisions at issue.
If the Park's clients who you have identified as sources of income are involved in
the ways listed in Regulation 18704.1(a), you might have a directly involved economic
interest. Based on your facts, this is not the case. The same wouli apply if the people
who are renting your rental units are involved in the governmental decisions in ihe ways
described in Regulation 18704.1(a). Based on the facis you have provided, you do not
appear to have a directly involved economic interest based on inctme from these sources.
For indirectly involved business entities, the materiality standard set forth in
Regulation 18705.1(c) applies. (Regulation 1g704.1(b).) (see Step 5 below for
discussion of the applicability of Regularion 1g705.1.)
Personal Financial Effects
A public official is directly involved in a govemmenral decision if the 4ecision
has any financial effect on his or her personal finances or those of his or her immediate
family. (Regulation 18704.5.) Again, your facts do not suggest that your personal
finances are involved with the governmental decision at isslle. If, however, yoLl are
making or participating in a decision that has any effect on your personal finances, the
conclusion would be different.
Step Five: Materiality Standard
A conflict of interest arises only when the reasonably foreseeable financial effect
of a governmental decision on a public official's economic interest is material.
(Regulation 18700(a).)
Real Property
The materiality standard for indirectly involved real property is set forth in
Regulation 18705.2(b)(1). The financial effect of a govemmental decision on indirectly
involved real property is presumed not to be material. This presumption may be rebutted
by proof that there are specific circumstances regarding the governmental decision, its
financial effect, and the nature of the real property in which the public official has an
economic interest that make it reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a
material financial effect on the real property in which the public official has in interest.
Based on the information you provided, the effect of the governmental decisions
on your rental properties would not be material.
Business Entity
For business entities not directly involved in the govemmental decision,
Regulation 18705.1 provides materialiiy standards that v-ary according to the financial
size of the business. For relatively small businesses (those with net income of less than
$500'000 for the most recent fiscal year), the materiality standards set forth in Regulation
18705.1(cX4) apply:
"(4) If the business entity is not covered by subdivisions (c)(l)-(3), the
financial effect of a govemmental decision on the business entity is
material if it is reasonably foreseeable that:
"(A) The governmental decision will result in an increase or decrease in
the business entity's gross revenues for a fiscal year in the amount of
$20,000 or more; or,
"(B) The governmental decision will result in the business entity incurring
or avoiding additional expenses or reducing or eliminating exisiing
expenses for a fiscal year in the amount of $5,000 or more; or,
"(C) The governmental decision will result in an increase or decrease in
the value of the business entity's assets or liabilities of $20,000 or more."
You have stated that the decisions that will come before the city council involve
mitigation efforts for a section of Needles that is abour 6-7 miles fromihe park. The city
council will not be deciding w,hether the casino will be built, but rather f the casino is
built, what effect it will have on Needles' population and services and what efforts will
need to be taken to mitigate the adverse effects. Because of the distance of the casino
from your business and the natllre of the decisions you will be making, it would appear
that there would be no material financial impact on the business. yoJ have not provided,
however, any specific information with regard to the governmental decisions
contemplated. Therefore, if you determine that the decisions before the Needles City
Council will have the effects listed above, then the effect of the governmental decisions
will be material.
Step Six: Reasonably Foreseeable
An effect upon economic interests is considered "reasonably foreseeable" if there
is a substantial likelihood that it will occur. (Regulation 18706(a).i Whether the
financial consequences of a governmental decision are substantially likely at the time the
decision is made depends on the facts surrounding the decision. A finaniial effect need
not be certain to be considered reasonably foreseeable, but it must be more than a mere
possibility. (ln re Thomer (1975) I FPPC Ops. 198.)
Ultimately, it is up to the public official to assess, through a good faith effort,
financial effects of the decision by using some reasonable ana ob.leciive method of
valuation. (Hensley Advice Letter, No. A-07-r 13; MoockAdvici Letter, No. A-01-140;
O'Harra Advice Letter, No. 4-00-174.) You will have to determine whether it is
reasonably foreseeable that the decisions before the city council will have a material
financial effect on any of your financial interests.
Steps Seven and Eight: Public Generally and Legally Required Participation
You have not presented any facts indicating that either the "public generally,'or
the "legally required participation" exception would be applicable hire. Accordingly, we
have not provided an analysis involving these steps

embedding.first_500_words: FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION 428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329 (916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886 September 22, 2008 Councilman Don McCone City of Needles 3520 Needles Hwy Needles, CA 92363 Re: Your Request for Advice Our File No. I-08-151 Dear Councilmember McCone: This letter responds to your request for advice regarding the conflict-of-interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹ You should not construe this letter as assessing any conduct that may have already taken place. (See Regulation 18329(b)(8)(A).) Because you seek general guidance, we provide you with informal assistance.² In addition, we base this letter on the facts presented; the Fair Political Practices Commission (the "Commission") does not act as a finder of fact when it renders assistance. (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71.) Please note that we base our advice solely on the provisions of the Act and offer no advice on how other state laws or local laws might affect you, including common law conflict of interest and Section 1090. We recommend that you seek advice from your agency's counsel on the application of conflict of interest laws outside the Act. QUESTION Does your ownership interest and management position in a Needles R.V. Park or your ownership of several rental homes present a disqualifying conflict of interest regarding your potential participation in the making of an intergovernmental agency agreement with an Indian tribe concerning public safety and other impacts that will be necessary if the voters approve an Indian gaming facility? ___________________________ ¹ The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated. ² Informal assistance does not offer the immunity provided by a Commission opinion or formal written advice. (Regulation 18329(c)(3) copy enclosed.) File No. I-08-151 Page No. 2 CONCLUSION Based on the facts as you have presented them, if you participate in making the intergovernmental agency agreement, there do not appear to be any reasonably foreseeable material financial effects on your economic interests that would create a disqualifying conflict of interest. As discussed below, however, once the detail and nature of the decisions to be made on the intergovernmental agency agreement become more specific, you will have to reassess whether you have a conflict of interest. FACTS You are a city councilmember for the city of Needles. On the local ballot in November, the people of Needles will decide whether to approve a new Indian gaming facility ("casino") outside the city limit along Interstate 40. An affirmative vote in November is necessary before the gaming facility can obtain final approval from the state or federal governments. After this, the city must enter into an inter-governmental agency agreement with the Indian tribe
